relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The ancient divorce law gives a written severing but leaves initiation in the husband's hand.
The woman may leave and form another household.
A second marriage may also end through rejection or death.
The first husband may not reclaim her after another marriage as though she were recoverable property.
A new husband is released from war and public burden to build joy at home.
A millstone cannot become collateral because food-making is life.
Kidnapping a person for sale or exploitation is treated as a capital crime.
Scale-disease is placed under careful priestly instruction rather than panic.
Miriam's story is remembered as warning and wound.
A creditor may not invade a debtor's home to seize security.
The debtor retains agency to bring the pledge outside.
Poverty limits what a creditor may hold overnight.
The garment returns by sunset so the poor person can sleep in warmth.
Citizen and immigrant worker receive the same protection from wage oppression.
Pay must arrive before sunset because a worker's life depends upon it.
Families may not be executed for one another's offenses.
The court may not bend against immigrant, orphan, or widow.
Exodus memory becomes a limit on economic power.
The forgotten sheaf becomes food intentionally left for the vulnerable.
The olive tree is not stripped to the last fruit.
The vineyard also retains a second harvest for others.
Again the reason is simple: remember slavery.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 24 converts memory into economic restraint. Do not enter the debtor's house. Do not hold the poor person's blanket. Do not delay wages. Do not strip the field bare. Freedom from Egypt becomes the neighbor's right to survive tonight.
